title: Thanksgiving Day
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
November 26, 2020 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on Thanksgiving Day (U.S.), November 26, 2020, which fell on a Thursday and was observed liturgically as such. The Episcopal Church uses the Revised Common Lectionary, and the appointed readings for Thanksgiving Day typically included Deuteronomy 8:7-18 (remembering provision in the wilderness), Colossians 3:12-17 (putting on virtues), and Luke 17:11-19 (the ten lepers and gratitude). The church was in Ordinary Time, specifically in the latter weeks before Advent (which would begin the following Sunday, November 29, 2020).

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the complexities of hybrid/virtual worship due to COVID-19 pandemic restrictions, with many cathedrals like Saint John's offering limited in-person and livestreamed services. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert Wright, was addressing racial justice commitments following the summer's Black Lives Matter movements and the church's resolutions on anti-racism passed at the 2018 General Convention. Broderick Greer, an African American priest and prominent voice in Episcopal circles for racial justice, was serving as Canon Missioner for Creation Care and Social Justice at Saint John's during this period.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was operating under COVID-19 restrictions that significantly limited gatherings; Colorado was experiencing a severe surge in cases in late November with hospitals approaching capacity. The week before Thanksgiving saw significant tension around holiday gathering guidelines, with Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment urging residents to limit gatherings despite the holiday. The Denver metro area, like much of the nation, was navigating political tensions following the November 3 presidential election, with some ongoing disputes over election integrity in Colorado (though the state was called decisively for Biden).

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was in the immediate aftermath of the 2020 presidential election (November 3), with President Trump contesting results and beginning legal challenges while mainstream media called the race for Joe Biden. The COVID-19 pandemic was at a critical juncture: the U.S. was approaching 250,000 deaths, daily cases were surging toward record levels, and the nation was divided over holiday gathering recommendations ahead of Thanksgiving. The Trump administration was in transition limbo, with the General Services Administration not yet formally recognizing the Biden transition team (Emily Murphy would certify transition on November 23, just days before this sermon). Black Lives Matter movements continued following months of protests over police brutality, with ongoing conversations about systemic racism and police reform.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 pandemic was ravaging Europe, with countries like France, Germany, and the UK implementing strict lockdowns; Europe had become a new pandemic epicenter with healthcare systems strained. The U.S. presidential election results were being closely watched internationally, particularly by allied nations concerned about American stability and foreign policy shifts under a Biden administration. The Middle East remained tense with ongoing concerns about Iran nuclear negotiations and the aftermath of the January killing of Iranian general Qasem Soleimani.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of profound anxiety and division: the election had been contentious, pandemic fatigue was acute after nine months of disruption, and the country was fractured along political and ideological lines regarding basic facts (election integrity, COVID severity). This was perhaps the most psychologically strained American Thanksgiving in decades, with many families unable to gather, holiday traditions canceled, and uncertainty about whether vaccines would arrive in time to change the trajectory of the pandemic. There was a particular poignancy to Thanksgiving 2020: gratitude practices were being promoted as mental health tools amid pervasive uncertainty and grief over pandemic deaths.
